replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
@Bunglefritz I agree.  I want "different".  I remember feeling the same way 20 odd years ago before streaming about TV output.  The BBC's content had become really stale.  Then all of a sudden they started producing a slew of new series.  They weren't all to my taste and I didn't watch them all but I appreciated the sheer variety.

We got the Doctor Who revival, Strictly Come Dancing, the brilliant Life On Mars and it's sequel Ashes To Ashes, Spooks, Hustle, Robin Hood, Merlin, a couple of Dickens adaptations presented in half hour episodes (Little Dorrit and Bleak House), Call The Midwife, Would I Lie To You?, the superb Line Of Duty and others.

In the last 10 years though everything's become very stale again.  Outside of the Eastenders and Casualty soap operas there's very little drama on the BBC in any given week and most of what there is consists of shows that have been running for well over a decade or spinoffs from them.  In fact that's true of most BBC content.

The lineup is virtually identical from one day to the next Monday to Friday.  (Some of those shows like Bargain Hunt and Escape To The Country are even shown on Saturday and Sunday too.) The primetime period between news bulletins that runs from 7pm to 10pm includes The One Show every day and Eastenders 4 out of 5 days.  That's essentially a third of primetime for the week.  If they're showing Masterchef or one of its spinoffs then that takes up another 2.5 hours in the week, so just 3 programmes account for half of primetime viewing.  It's risible, which is why I hardly ever watch the BBC these days.

According to feminist values however, the girl boss is amazing simply because she exists and should never be asked to prove herself. She is better than everyone and she lets everyone know by taking every opportunity to condescend to them. She aspires to be the boss out of sheer ego because, "she deserves it". She does not need to sacrifice herself because she doesn't owe anybody anything. In short, feminist values are those of a villain. That is the struggle of the girl boss. Well the thing is, we didn't.  A "girl boss" is a derogatory term for a supposed female action hero who's whiny, weak and essentially a loud-mouthed female activist who squares off agains thte supposed patriarchy, etc.  A normal, well-written female character in an action movie is not a "girl boss". You have plenty of strong female characters in Abyss, Predator, True Lies, etc. etc. etc.  Even sillier films like all of the Resident Evil movies.  They're garbage action schlock...but never once does one of the female characters gripe about being "disenfranchised" or "struggling to become a zombie killer because I'm a woman!", etc.

replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
The poor standard of recent releases and the messaging shoehorned in to them has certainly exacerbated it, but Superhero fatigue is real.  It's inevitable when there have been over 70 movies and numerous TV/streaming series amounting to thousands of hours over the last 20 years that fatigue would set in.  Superhero stories, like any genre, have to follow the tenets that make a genre what it is and a point will always be reached where you run thin on how many different ways you can basically tell the same type of story.

There's still room for superhero movies but it's become an oversaturated market and only a few will now be successful at the box office.  Those are most likely to be the ones where there is strong character recognition amongst the general public such as Superman, Batman and Spider-man.  Folk are much less likely now to go and see a movie about yet another superhero they've never heard of.

Most people didn't go to see a movie like The Eternals because of all the gender and race swapping of the characters from the comics.  Most people wouldn't have even be aware that had happened because they've not even heard of them ad that's the reason why they didn't go.  They were just not interested in 10 more superpowered characters in a franchise that already has dozens of them.

author: @Oraoraoraaaaa
text:
Gotta say something about The Wheel Of Time that I think is commonly misunderstood by people who only watched the show: the books are girlbossy themselves too, because society in the story is inherently misandric. The difference lies within the details. Jordan was able to depict a feminine ruled society that made sense, and that's because the character traits from everyone in the books felt natural and believable, and the societal model was a logical consequence of previous events, not something that was imposed or fought for.

Power and relevance do not come from masculine traits, they are achieved by taking advantage of your strengths, whichever they are. This is made clear all throughout the books, Perrin's loyalty, Mat's carefree chaos, Rand's perseverance are just as capital to the story as Elayne's drive, Egwene's adaptability or Nynaeve's emotions. The same works for the negative traits too. Men are percieved by most women as simple, immature, violent brutes while women are percieved by most men as bossy, unpredictable, devious snakes. And part of the charm about it is how wrong they are about each other and the huge amount of trouble these kind of situations trigger. Jordan's message - in my opinion anyway - is the need to come to terms, the importance of recognising the other's value and work together to get the world back to a great place. 

Also, the power dynamics are extremely interesting to analyze, because they differ a lot between men, women and when men or women have to face an individual from the opposite sex.

Obviously and for political reasons, this is not portrayed the same way in the series, and we just end up watching the bits that are useful to the message.

PS: Sorry if my english is not perfect, it's not my first language

replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
There's absolutely superhero fatigue.  There have been so many movies and TV/streaming shows since Iron Man was released in 2008 it was inevitable.  We're talking 70+ movies and thousands of hours on the small screen.  Every genre has adhere to a certain format, a certain structure.  Those are what make them what they are.  There are only so many ways of telling the same basic stories.  Eventually one superhero tale becomes much like another.

How many Marvel movies have a superhero and therefore a supervillain who can pose a threat to them, big fight scenes, lots of explosions and huge damage to the surrounding area etc. with the hero eventually triumphing?  After a while it all becomes too familiar and folk get bored and stop going to them all.

Instead they stick to their favourites, which in most cases are characters that are well known such as Spider-Man or at least ones they've seen in previous movies like Doctor Strange or The Guardians of the Galaxy.  They don't bother to turn out for the likes of The Eternals, Shang-Chi or The Thunderbolts.

replies.author: @MaximRecoil
replies.text:
@jjc5407 "There have been so many movies and TV/streaming shows since Iron Man was released in 2008 it was inevitable.  We're talking 70+ movies and thousands of hours on the small screen."

There have been 36 MCU movies, with Iron Man (2008) being the first one. The "70" number is for all movies based on a Marvel comic, starting with Howard the Duck (1986).

replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
@MaximRecoil I wasn't just referring to Marvel, but superhero content in general.

There's been a couple of dozen DC movies in that time plus all the Sony X-Men and Spider-Man and associated spin off movies.

Then there are other superhero genre movies that are not are not based on Marvel or DC comics.

On the small screen DC have given us the tail end of Smallville (the final 65 episodes were broadcast from 2008 onwards), 5 seasons of Gotham, all the Arrow-verse series, Titans on Netflix etc.

Marvel had several series on Netflix before Disney+ picked up the mantel and started churning out shows.

And again there have been other superhero genre series including the likes of Heroes and Alphas.

And that's just live action.  There's also been numerous animated films and series too.
